item.title: Critical daily-fetch bug fixed in github-weekly-reporter
item.description:
Resolved a data loss issue where daily-fetch collected the previous week's events, causing Sunday's data to be permanently lost.
This week was consumed by stabilizing the github-weekly-reporter tool, particularly fixing a critical bug in the daily-fetch process that caused event data loss. The bug meant that on Mondays, the week ID would flip before Sunday's events were collected, resulting in missing data for entire weeks. Multiple related fixes were implemented to ensure reliable weekly reporting.
Alongside the bug fixes, I managed a large-scale dependency update effort across 10+ repositories using Dependabot, and significantly improved test coverage in the main repo from 40% to 63%. Commit activity was bursty, with two major peaks on April 1st (161 commits) and April 4th (283 commits) corresponding to intensive debugging and merge sessions.
The focus shifted from feature development to robustness and maintenance, preparing the tool for public release. The combination of bug fixes, automation, and testing has put the project in a much healthier state.
Commits: 312, PRs: 53, Reviews: 53

item.title: CSS View Transitions API: fixing flash and adding smooth fades in my blog
item.description:
Implemented smooth page transitions using the View Transitions API and debugged a flash issue caused by mismatched background colors and transition durations.
The week exploded with 120 commits and 68 reviews, starting with a massive dependency update sprint across 8 different repositories. I automated Ruby and Rust dependency bumps for both my own projects and several external tooling repos, handling multiple version bumps for `ffi`, `parser`, `insta`, and `uuid`.
The narrative pivot came when I shifted from maintenance to user experience work on my blog. I implemented the CSS View Transitions API for smooth cross-page fades, but immediately hit a visual bug: pages flashed black during transitions. Debugging revealed mismatched background colors between `html` and `body` elements combined with asymmetric fade durations (0.2s out, 0.3s in) created a transparency window.
I wrapped the week by writing a reflective Japanese blog post about my OSS journey during unemployment, sharing the three principles that guided my 6,000-star project suite. The open PR in `ashkulz/committers.top` to fix user search by switching to REST API also continued to evolve, highlighting my cross-organization contributions beyond my own ecosystem.
Commits: 120, PRs: 5, Reviews: 68

item.title: Markdown Diff and History Tabs Merge into mdts
item.description:
Implemented new tabs for real-time diff and commit history in the markdown tool, enhancing workflow.
This week was a balancing act between routine dependency maintenance and a significant feature launch in my core project, mdts. I spent considerable time ensuring various repositories stayed up-to-date with security patches, while also pushing through a long-planned enhancement to the markdown editing workflow.
The centerpiece was merging the 'feat: add markdown diff view and previous commit history tabs' pull request, which adds Diff and History tabs to the markdown content view. This required careful integration of git commands for diff output and building a responsive UI with color-coded rendering. Simultaneously, I processed a wave of dependency updates across Ruby, Java, and JavaScript projects, including bumps to nokogiri, gradle-wrapper, and json libraries.
The week wrapped up with an open pull request in ashkulz/committers.top, where I'm fixing a GraphQL API misuse that causes incorrect user sorting. This cross-repo collaboration underscores the interconnected nature of open source maintenance beyond my own repositories.
Commits: 64, PRs: 3, Reviews: 42

item.title: tree-sitter-dart 0.1.0 upgrade in gitlogue opens amid 19 dependency merges
item.description:
Major grammar bump for Dart parsing alongside cross-org maintenance across 14 repositories
This week was dominated by dependency maintenance across my personal projects and several external organizations. I opened a significant PR bumping tree-sitter-dart from 0.0.4 to 0.1.0 in gitlogue—a jump that promises improved Dart parsing—while simultaneously merging 19 other dependency updates spanning Rust, JavaScript, and Ruby ecosystems.
The tree-sitter-dart upgrade is particularly interesting because it's not just a patch version bump; 0.1.0 likely includes breaking changes or major features that will affect how gitlogue processes Dart repositories. Meanwhile, my contributions to bitflyer-tools, circleci-tools, and irasutoya-tools show how dependency maintenance often crosses organizational boundaries, with the same clap/rubocop-ast updates appearing in multiple places.
With 61 commits and 59 reviews, I stayed busy both pushing changes and reviewing others' work. The pattern emerges clearly: one substantial open PR for a language grammar upgrade, plus a flood of smaller, automated dependency bumps that keep the toolchain healthy across all these projects.
Commits: 61, PRs: 0, Reviews: 59
